alert.title: If you are currently detained under Section 3
points: You are entitled to a FREE Independent Mental Health Advocate (IMHA) — ask ward staff immediately.
Your nearest relative can write to the Hospital Managers ordering discharge under s.23 — see the Letter Generator tab.
You can apply to the Mental Health Tribunal — an independent judicial body that can order your release.
On discharge from s.3 you are entitled to free aftercare (s.117) — the local authority cannot charge you.

orgChart.title: NHS Trust Board
orgChart.subtitle: Chair + Non-Executive Directors
orgChart.relevance: Holds ultimate accountability. Hospital Managers under the MHA are delegated from this body.

children.title: Chief Executive Officer
children.subtitle: Trust CEO
children.relevance: Leads the trust operationally. Rarely involved in individual patient decisions.

children.title: Medical Director
children.subtitle: Executive Medical Lead
children.relevance: Senior clinician overseeing all medical practice. May be involved in complex MHA disputes.

children.title: Clinical Director
children.subtitle: Per Division (e.g. Acute, Forensic, CAMHS)
children.relevance: Leads a clinical division. Oversees Responsible Clinicians within their division.

children.title: Responsible Clinician (RC)
children.subtitle: Consultant Psychiatrist (usually)
children.relevance: KEY PERSON — Holds your Section 3. Can bar nearest relative discharge under s.25. Can discharge you under s.23.

children.title: Junior Doctors / Specialty Registrars
children.subtitle: SHO, SpR, CT1–3, ST4–6
children.relevance: Day-to-day medical reviews. Write notes and recommendations but RC has final say.

children.title: Director of Nursing
children.subtitle: Chief Nurse / Executive Nurse
children.relevance: Leads all nursing staff. Complaints about ward nursing care go through this chain.

children.title: Modern Matron
children.subtitle: Covers multiple wards
children.relevance: Oversees ward managers. Good escalation point for ward-level concerns.

children.title: Ward Manager
children.subtitle: Band 7 Nurse
children.relevance: Runs the ward day-to-day. Manages staffing, ward rules, and immediate concerns.

children.title: Staff Nurses
children.subtitle: Band 5–6 (RMN qualified)
children.relevance: Day-to-day nursing care. Named Nurse is your primary nursing contact.

children.title: Healthcare Assistants
children.subtitle: Band 2–3
children.relevance: Support workers on the ward. Observations, activities, general support.

children.title: Director of Operations
children.subtitle: COO equivalent
children.relevance: Oversees operational delivery. Bed management and service capacity sit here.

children.title: Service Manager
children.subtitle: Per service line
children.relevance: Manages non-clinical operations for a group of wards/services.

children.title: MHA Administration / Hospital Managers Office
children.subtitle: Mental Health Act Office
children.relevance: CRITICAL — Processes s.23 nearest relative discharge notices. Organises Hospital Managers Hearings. Your mum's letter goes HERE.

children.title: Hospital Managers Panel
children.subtitle: Non-exec / lay panel members
children.relevance: Can discharge you independently. Hear cases when RC bars nearest relative discharge or on routine review.

children.title: Allied Health Professionals
children.subtitle: Multidisciplinary Team (MDT)
children.relevance: Part of your care team. Their input informs RC decisions and tribunal reports.

children.title: Clinical Psychologist
children.subtitle: Therapy & assessment
children.relevance: Psychological formulation and therapy. Often provides evidence for tribunals.

children.title: Social Worker / AMHP
children.subtitle: Approved Mental Health Professional
children.relevance: AMHPs made the original s.3 application. Social workers support discharge planning and community care.

children.title: Occupational Therapist
children.subtitle: OT
children.relevance: Assesses functional ability. Reports on daily living skills inform discharge readiness.

children.title: Independent Oversight
children.subtitle: External to the Trust
children.relevance: Bodies that oversee the trust from outside. Important if you feel the trust isn't following the law.

children.title: Care Quality Commission (CQC)
children.subtitle: Regulator & Inspector
children.relevance: Regulates and inspects. You can complain to CQC. They also manage SOADs for treatment.

children.title: Independent Mental Health Advocate (IMHA)
children.subtitle: Free advocacy service
children.relevance: YOU ARE ENTITLED TO THIS. Free advocacy to help you understand and exercise your rights under the MHA.

children.title: Mental Health Tribunal
children.subtitle: First-tier Tribunal (Health, Education & Social Care)
children.relevance: JUDICIAL BODY — Can order your discharge. You can apply once per detention period. Nearest relative can apply if RC bars discharge.

documents.name: Observation Record Sheet

mhaSections.title: Admission for Assessment
mhaSections.purpose: Admission and detention for assessment (and treatment) of mental disorder.
mhaSections.whoApplies: AMHP + 2 doctors (at least 1 s.12 approved). Nearest relative can also apply.
keyPoints: Cannot be renewed — must convert to s.3 or discharge
Nearest relative can apply to Tribunal within 14 days
Automatic referral to Tribunal if not applied within 14 days
Nearest relative can object to admission (AMHP must consult)
IMHA entitlement applies
s.132 rights must be given on admission

mhaSections.title: Admission for Treatment
mhaSections.purpose: Detention for treatment of mental disorder. The primary long-term detention power.
mhaSections.whoApplies: AMHP + 2 doctors (at least 1 s.12 approved). Nearest relative cannot be overruled if they object (unlike s.2).
keyPoints: Renewable for a further 6 months, then yearly
Nearest relative can order discharge (s.23) — but RC can bar (s.25)
Can apply to Tribunal once per detention period
Nearest relative can apply if RC bars discharge (s.25)
Triggers s.117 aftercare entitlement on discharge
IMHA entitlement applies
AMHP must consult nearest relative and cannot proceed if NR objects (unless displaced)

mhaSections.title: Emergency Admission for Assessment
mhaSections.purpose: Emergency admission where s.2 cannot be arranged urgently. Short-term bridge only.
mhaSections.whoApplies: AMHP (or nearest relative) + 1 doctor (does not need to be s.12 approved)
keyPoints: Used only when s.2 urgently necessary and 2 doctors cannot attend
Should be converted to s.2 or s.3 within 72 hours
No Tribunal right for s.4 itself (only after conversion)
Rarely used in practice

mhaSections.title: Doctor's Holding Power
mhaSections.purpose: Emergency power to detain an informal inpatient while a formal assessment is arranged.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Doctor in charge of patient's treatment (or nominated deputy)
keyPoints: Only applies to patients already admitted as informal inpatients
Triggers formal MHA assessment within 72 hours
Cannot be renewed
Does not allow treatment without consent
Must be reported on Form H4

mhaSections.title: Nurse's Holding Power
mhaSections.purpose: Allows a qualified mental health nurse to detain an informal patient for up to 6 hours when a doctor cannot attend immediately.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Qualified mental health nurse (RMN) — no doctor required
keyPoints: Only for informal inpatients
Ends when doctor arrives and assesses, or after 6 hours
Nurse must record on Form H5
Does not authorise treatment
Doctor should then apply s.5(2) or formal section if needed

mhaSections.title: Leave of Absence
mhaSections.purpose: Grants a detained patient permission to leave hospital. Leave conditions are set by the RC.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Granted by Responsible Clinician only
keyPoints: RC sets conditions (escorted/unescorted, destination, duration)
Can be recalled from leave at any time
Leave > 7 days triggers CTO consideration
Extended s.17 leave (over 7 days) requires CTO unless on s.17 leave
Leave can be cancelled by RC at any time

mhaSections.title: Community Treatment Order (CTO)
mhaSections.purpose: Allows a patient to live in the community under conditions, with power of recall to hospital.
mhaSections.whoApplies: RC makes the order with AMHP agreement
keyPoints: Patient must have been detained under s.3 (or equivalent)
Conditions set by RC — e.g., residence, medication, attendance
Can be recalled to hospital if conditions breached or risk increases
Nearest relative can discharge from CTO (subject to barring)
Tribunal right applies
IMHA entitlement applies

mhaSections.title: Return of Absent Without Leave (AWOL)
mhaSections.purpose: Authorises the return to hospital of a detained patient who has absconded.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Any authorised member of hospital staff, police, or AMHP
keyPoints: Applies when patient leaves without authorisation or fails to return from leave
Police can enter premises to return AWOL patient
AWOL period does not count towards detention duration
If AWOL for 28+ days, detention may lapse (time limits apply)

mhaSections.title: Discharge
mhaSections.purpose: Power to discharge a detained patient. Can be exercised by RC, Hospital Managers, or Nearest Relative.
mhaSections.whoApplies: RC / Hospital Managers / Nearest Relative
keyPoints: Nearest Relative must give 72 hours written notice (to allow s.25 barring)
RC can immediately discharge without notice
Hospital Managers can discharge after a hearing
Nearest Relative discharge cannot be unreasonably withheld
Use s.23(2)(a) — Nearest Relative written notice to Hospital Managers

mhaSections.title: Report Barring Discharge by Nearest Relative
mhaSections.purpose: RC can file a report preventing the nearest relative from discharging the patient for 6 months.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Responsible Clinician (within 72 hours of NR discharge notice)
keyPoints: RC must believe patient would be dangerous to self or others if discharged
RC has 72 hours from NR notice to file a s.25 report
If barred, nearest relative can apply to Mental Health Tribunal
Barring period is 6 months (cannot give another s.23 notice during this period)
Hospital Managers receive the s.25 report — recorded on Form H6

mhaSections.title: Transfer from Prison
mhaSections.purpose: s.47: Transfer of sentenced prisoner to hospital. s.48: Transfer of remand prisoner to hospital.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Secretary of State for Justice (on recommendation of 2 doctors)
keyPoints: s.47 — sentenced prisoners transferred for treatment
s.48 — remand prisoners (used for more urgent cases)
Often paired with a restriction order (s.49)
Patient retains prisoner legal status
Tribunal rights apply

mhaSections.title: Treatment Requiring Consent or Second Opinion
mhaSections.purpose: Protects patients from certain treatments (especially long-term medication) without consent or independent doctor review.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Applies after 3 months of treatment under detention
keyPoints: After 3 months, RC must certify patient consents OR obtain SOAD certificate
SOAD (Second Opinion Appointed Doctor) appointed by CQC
SOAD independently reviews whether treatment is appropriate
Applies to medication — not ECT (see s.58A)
Patient can withdraw consent at any time

mhaSections.title: Electro-convulsive Therapy (ECT)
mhaSections.purpose: Special safeguards for ECT — stricter than s.58. Requires consent or SOAD certificate.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Any detained patient (no 3-month period)
keyPoints: ECT cannot be given without consent if patient has capacity
If patient lacks capacity, SOAD must certify it does not conflict with advance decision or attorney
Stricter than s.58 — applies immediately, not after 3 months

mhaSections.title: After-care
mhaSections.purpose: Duty on NHS and local authority to provide after-care for patients discharged from s.3 (and equivalents). FREE — cannot be charged for.
mhaSections.whoApplies: NHS and Local Authority jointly responsible
keyPoints: Applies on discharge from s.3, s.37, s.47, s.48 (and equivalents)
Services are FREE — local authority cannot charge for s.117 services
Continues until both NHS and LA agree it is no longer needed
Covers housing, support, mental health services, medication
Joint planning required before discharge

mhaSections.title: Independent Mental Health Advocate (IMHA)
mhaSections.purpose: Statutory right to an independent advocate for detained patients and those on CTOs.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Free service — available to all qualifying patients
keyPoints: Entitlement under ss.130A–130D MHA (inserted by MHA 2007)
IMHA can attend meetings, review records, help patient understand rights
Must be told about this right on admission (s.132)
To access: ask ward staff for referral to IMHA service
Completely independent of the hospital trust

mhaSections.title: Duty to Give Information
mhaSections.purpose: Hospital must tell you about your rights when you are detained. This includes Tribunal rights, IMHA, and how to get help.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Hospital (duty on managers)
keyPoints: Must be given as soon as practicable after detention
Must be given verbally AND in writing
Covers: reason for detention, Tribunal rights, IMHA, consent to treatment, discharge
Failure to provide rights information is a legal breach
Information must be in appropriate language/format

mhaSections.title: Warrant to Enter Premises
mhaSections.purpose: Allows police to enter premises (with AMHP and doctor) to remove a person believed to have a mental disorder.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Police (with magistrate warrant, applied for by AMHP)
keyPoints: Magistrate issues warrant on AMHP application
Police enter with AMHP and doctor present
Person can be removed to a place of safety
Used when access is refused or person is alone and unable to care for self

mhaSections.title: Police Power: Place of Safety
mhaSections.purpose: Police can detain someone in a public place who appears to have a mental disorder, for assessment at a place of safety.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Police constable — no warrant or doctor needed
keyPoints: Only applies in public places
Person taken to designated place of safety (usually hospital or suite)
Must be assessed by doctor and AMHP within the period
Maximum 24 hours — extendable by 12 hours if doctor certifies
Not a formal detention — no Tribunal right
Custody suite rarely used now — hospital preferred

mhaSections.title: Definition of Nearest Relative
mhaSections.purpose: Defines who counts as a patient's 'Nearest Relative' (NR) — the person with statutory powers to discharge, object to admission, and apply to the Tribunal.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Applies automatically to all detained patients — no application needed.
keyPoints: Hierarchy (highest priority first): spouse/civil partner or cohabitee (6+ months); child; parent; sibling; grandparent; grandchild; uncle/aunt; nephew/niece; other relative
Cohabiting partner of 6+ months ranks equal to spouse (and above all others if no spouse)
Oldest person in a tie takes priority
Patient can nominate someone else to act as NR — but this is not legally binding
If NR is under 18, moves abroad, or is themselves a patient, they are passed over
Estranged nearest relatives still count unless displaced by a court (s.29)

mhaSections.title: Displacement of Nearest Relative
mhaSections.purpose: Allows a court to appoint a different person to act as Nearest Relative if the existing NR is unsuitable, unreasonably objects to discharge, or has abandoned the patient.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Application to County Court by: AMHP, any relative, anyone the patient lives with, or a local authority.
keyPoints: Grounds for displacement: NR is incapable of acting; NR has unreasonably objected to a s.3 application; NR would exercise discharge power without due regard to patient's welfare or the public interest; NR has abandoned the patient
Court appoints an 'Acting Nearest Relative' — usually the AMHP or another relative
Process takes weeks — not suitable if urgent discharge is needed
Patient themselves can ask the AMHP to apply for NR displacement
If NR was barred from discharging (s.25), they may still be displaced if the barring reveals unreasonableness
A displaced NR loses all MHA powers until the court restores them

mhaSections.title: Applications to the Mental Health Tribunal
mhaSections.purpose: Sets out who can apply to the First-tier Tribunal (Mental Health) and the strict time windows within which applications must be made.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Patient, Nearest Relative (in limited circumstances), or via automatic referral (s.68).
keyPoints: s.2 patient: must apply within 14 days of detention. One application only.
s.3 patient: one application per detention period (6 months or 12 months at renewal)
CTO patient: one application per CTO period
Nearest Relative barred from discharge (s.25): NR can apply to Tribunal within 28 days of the barring report
If a patient has not applied, hospital must refer automatically under s.68
Missing the window is fatal — you cannot apply outside it (for that period)
Application is free; legal aid is automatically available for the hearing

mhaSections.title: Automatic Referral to Tribunal
mhaSections.purpose: Requires the hospital to automatically refer a patient to the Tribunal if the patient has not exercised their right to apply — a safety net to ensure every patient gets Tribunal review.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Hospital Managers must refer. No action needed by the patient.
keyPoints: s.2: automatic referral if patient has not applied within 14 days
s.3: automatic referral after 6 months in hospital without a Tribunal (if not referred or heard)
s.3 renewal: automatic referral each time the section is renewed if no Tribunal in 3 years
CTO: automatic referral if no Tribunal within 6 months of CTO
This right cannot be waived or blocked by the hospital
Even without legal aid or a solicitor, the hospital must make this referral

mhaSections.title: Tribunal Powers: The Discharge Test
mhaSections.purpose: Sets out the legal test the Tribunal applies to decide whether to discharge — and critically, the burden of proof is on the HOSPITAL to justify continued detention, NOT on the patient to prove they should be released.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Applied by First-tier Tribunal (Mental Health) at every hearing.
keyPoints: BURDEN OF PROOF: The hospital must prove continued detention is justified. The patient does not have to prove they should be free.

The Tribunal MUST discharge if it cannot be satisfied that: (a) the patient is suffering from mental disorder of a nature or degree which makes detention appropriate; AND (b) detention is necessary for the patient's health or safety or protection of others; AND (c) appropriate treatment is available
For s.2: also must be satisfied the admission is justified for assessment
Tribunal CAN (but need not) discharge if criteria are met but circumstances otherwise justify it
Tribunal can order: absolute discharge; conditional discharge (with conditions); deferred discharge (discharge on a future date); recommendation for leave, transfer, or supervised discharge
Conditional discharge: patient released with conditions (e.g., medication, supervision) — breach allows recall to hospital
If ANY ONE of the three criteria cannot be proven by the hospital, the Tribunal must discharge

mhaSections.title: Restricted Patient Discharge (s.37/41)
mhaSections.purpose: Sets out the discharge rules for patients subject to a restriction order (s.41) — a completely different pathway requiring Ministry of Justice involvement.
mhaSections.whoApplies: Applies to patients admitted under s.37 hospital order with a s.41 restriction order (almost always forensic/criminal justice patients).
keyPoints: Tribunal can grant CONDITIONAL discharge — patient released but under supervision conditions
Tribunal can grant ABSOLUTE discharge — patient fully free, but only if the Tribunal is satisfied no conditions are needed
Absolute discharge very rare — usually given only after a conditional discharge period has been successful
The Secretary of State (Ministry of Justice) can independently grant conditional or absolute discharge at any time
The MoJ can grant supervised leave (s.17) as a step toward discharge — progress through leave is essential
Conditional discharge can be recalled if conditions are breached or risk increases — recalled directly to hospital
Restricted patients must apply to Tribunal annually; automatic referral applies if no hearing in 3 years

letterTemplate.title: Section 23 Nearest Relative Discharge Notice
letterTemplate.citation: Mental Health Act 1983, c.20, ss.23, 25. Available at: https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1983/20/section/23 (Accessed: 27 February 2026).

fields.label: Nearest relative's full name
fields.hint: The person writing the letter (nearest relative)

fields.label: Hospital name
fields.placeholder: e.g. St. Elsewhere NHS Foundation Trust

templateLines: Re: Notice of Discharge under Section 23 of the Mental Health Act 1983
I am writing in my capacity as the nearest relative of the above-named patient, who is currently detained under Section 3 of the Mental Health Act 1983.

In accordance with my rights under Section 23(2)(a) of the Mental Health Act 1983, I hereby give written notice that I am ordering the discharge of the patient from detention. I understand that this notice provides for a period of 72 hours during which the responsible clinician may, if appropriate, furnish a report under Section 25 barring the discharge.

I believe that the criteria for continued detention under Section 3 are no longer met, and that my relative can be appropriately supported in the community. I respectfully request that this notice be acknowledged in writing and that I am informed of any decision made by the responsible clinician in response.
Please do not hesitate to contact me should you require any further information.
Mental Health Act 1983, c.20, ss.23, 25. Available at: https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1983/20/section/23 (Accessed: 27 February 2026).

rights.title: Independent Mental Health Advocate (IMHA)
rights.summary: You have a legal right to a FREE, independent advocate under s.130A MHA 1983.
rights.detail: An IMHA is completely independent of the hospital. They can attend ward round meetings with you, help you understand your rights, assist you in communicating your views, and support you in challenging your detention. This right applies the moment you are detained under s.2 or s.3.
rights.howToExercise: Ask any ward staff member to refer you to the IMHA service. This cannot be refused. You can also search for your local IMHA provider online.
rights.legalBasis: Mental Health Act 1983, ss.130A–130D (inserted by Mental Health Act 2007, s.30)

rights.title: Right to Apply to a Mental Health Tribunal
rights.summary: You can apply to an independent judicial tribunal that has the power to order your release.
rights.detail: The Mental Health Tribunal is a judicial body — completely independent of the NHS. It includes a judge, a consultant psychiatrist, and a lay member. It can order your discharge even if the RC disagrees. Under s.3, you can apply once in the first 6 months of detention. Your nearest relative can also apply if the RC has barred their discharge notice (s.25).
rights.howToExercise: Contact the Tribunal Service directly, or ask your solicitor or IMHA to make the application on your behalf. Legal aid is available for Tribunal hearings.

rights.title: Nearest Relative's Right to Discharge (s.23)
rights.summary: Your nearest relative can write to the Hospital Managers ordering your discharge from s.3 detention.
rights.detail: Under s.23(2)(a), the nearest relative can give written notice to the Hospital Managers ordering discharge. The hospital then has 72 hours in which the RC can file a s.25 barring report. If no barring report is filed within 72 hours, you must be discharged. If the RC does bar the discharge, the nearest relative can apply to the Mental Health Tribunal.
rights.howToExercise: Nearest relative sends a signed written letter to the Hospital Managers / MHA Administration office. Keep a copy and send recorded delivery. The 72-hour clock starts on receipt.

rights.title: Hospital Managers' Hearing
rights.summary: You can request a hearing before the Hospital Managers Panel — an independent panel that can discharge you.
rights.detail: Hospital Managers are lay people (non-clinical, non-executive) who hold delegated power to discharge detained patients under s.23. You can request a hearing at any time, and one must be offered when your detention is renewed. The panel reviews your case and can order discharge even if the RC disagrees.
rights.howToExercise: Write to the MHA Administration Office requesting a Hospital Managers Hearing. You are entitled to bring a solicitor or advocate.
rights.legalBasis: Mental Health Act 1983, s.23(2)(b); Code of Practice ch.38

rights.title: Right to Be Told Your Rights (s.132)
rights.summary: The hospital MUST tell you about your rights as soon as practicable after detention — verbally and in writing.
rights.detail: Under s.132, the hospital has a duty to explain to you: why you are detained, your right to apply to the Tribunal, your right to an IMHA, how treatment can be given, how to make a complaint, and how to contact a solicitor. If you were not given this information, this is a breach of the MHA.
rights.howToExercise: If you were not given this information, raise it with your IMHA or solicitor. It can be relevant in Tribunal proceedings.

rights.title: Free After-care (s.117)
rights.summary: After discharge from s.3, you are entitled to FREE after-care services jointly funded by the NHS and local authority.
rights.detail: Under s.117, the NHS and local authority have a joint duty to provide after-care to patients discharged from s.3 (and equivalents) until they are satisfied the services are no longer needed. Critically, this is FREE — the local authority cannot charge for s.117 services (confirmed by the House of Lords in R v Manchester City Council ex p Stennett). This covers mental health support, housing assistance, and community services.
rights.howToExercise: A s.117 aftercare meeting should be held before discharge. If not arranged, raise it with the social worker or IMHA. Challenge any attempt to charge for s.117 services.
rights.legalBasis: Mental Health Act 1983, s.117; R v Manchester CC ex p Stennett [2002] UKHL 34

rights.title: Right to Refuse Treatment (s.58 / s.58A)
rights.summary: After 3 months, medication requires your consent OR an independent SOAD certificate. ECT always requires consent if you have capacity.
rights.detail: For the first 3 months of detention, medication can be given without consent under the MHA. After 3 months, the RC must either certify that you consent, or obtain a Second Opinion Appointed Doctor (SOAD) certificate from the CQC. For ECT (electroconvulsive therapy), consent is always required if you have capacity — there is no 3-month period.
rights.howToExercise: Tell your RC or named nurse that you withdraw consent. For ECT, clearly state your refusal. Seek IMHA support if needed.

rights.title: Mental Capacity Rights (MCA 2005)
rights.summary: Detention under the MHA does not remove your mental capacity. You still have rights under the Mental Capacity Act 2005.
rights.detail: Mental capacity is decision-specific. You may have capacity to make some decisions but not others. The MCA presumes you have capacity unless proven otherwise. Any decision made on your behalf must be in your best interests, using the least restrictive option. You can make an Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment (ADRT) which is legally binding even under detention (for physical health decisions).
rights.howToExercise: If you believe a capacity assessment has been done incorrectly, challenge it. Ask for an IMHA or IMCA (Independent Mental Capacity Advocate) if applicable.
rights.legalBasis: Mental Capacity Act 2005, ss.1–4; Code of Practice

rights.title: Right to Complain
rights.summary: You can complain about your treatment. Multiple routes exist: PALS, formal NHS complaint, CQC, and the Parliamentary Ombudsman.
rights.detail: PALS (Patient Advice and Liaison Service) handles informal concerns. Formal complaints go to the trust's complaints manager. If unsatisfied, escalate to the Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman. The CQC inspects mental health services and you can report concerns to them. These complaints can be made by you, family, or your IMHA.
rights.howToExercise: Ask ward staff for PALS contact details. Formal complaints must be acknowledged within 3 working days and responded to within 25 working days.
rights.legalBasis: NHS Complaints Regulations 2009; CQC: Health and Social Care Act 2008

rights.title: Right to Legal Representation
rights.summary: You have the right to consult a solicitor in private. Legal aid is available for Mental Health Tribunal cases.
rights.detail: You can see a solicitor in private at any time — this cannot be restricted. Legal aid (public funding) is available for Mental Health Tribunal representation. Your IMHA or a ward nurse can help you contact a mental health law solicitor. The Law Society has a specialist mental health panel of accredited solicitors.
rights.howToExercise: Ask ward staff for a private phone to call a solicitor. Search the Law Society's mental health panel directory. Your IMHA can help arrange this.
rights.legalBasis: Mental Health Act 1983, s.134(3); Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012

rights.title: Right to Access Your Medical Records (Subject Access Request)
rights.summary: You can request a copy of ALL your medical records held by the NHS trust — including previous admission notes, risk assessments, RC reports, and any documents that will be presented to the Tribunal.
rights.detail: Under UK GDPR (Art.15) you have the right to a copy of all personal data held about you. This includes ward observation logs, risk assessments, previous section documents, and any clinical records that will be used in Tribunal proceedings. The trust must respond within ONE MONTH. You can also request that inaccurate information be corrected under Art.16. Knowing what is in your records before a Tribunal is a strategic advantage — your solicitor will need this too.
rights.howToExercise: Write to the Trust's Data Protection / Medical Records department by email or recorded letter with: your name, DOB, NHS number, ward, and what you want. Request both current and historical records. Say it is a Subject Access Request under UK GDPR Art.15. It is FREE. If refused or delayed, complain to the ICO (ico.org.uk).
rights.legalBasis: UK General Data Protection Regulation, Art.15 (right of access), Art.16 (right to rectification); Data Protection Act 2018

rights.title: Challenge Renewal of Section 3
rights.summary: Every time your s.3 detention is renewed (after 6 months, then annually), a fresh legal test applies — and you have a fresh right to apply to the Tribunal.
rights.detail: At renewal, the RC must prepare a renewal report under s.20 and serve it on the Hospital Managers. The Managers must review it and can discharge the patient themselves. More importantly, renewal triggers a new period for Tribunal application. The RC must also inform you of the renewal and your rights. Renewal is NOT automatic — the Managers can refuse to renew. If you have had no Tribunal hearing in the past 3 years, the Managers must also refer you automatically (s.68).
rights.howToExercise: When informed of renewal, immediately contact your solicitor and apply to the Tribunal for the new period. Ask the Hospital Managers to review the renewal report themselves. If the RC's renewal report contains inaccuracies, submit a written response to the Managers.
rights.legalBasis: Mental Health Act 1983, ss.20, 23(2)(b), 68; Code of Practice ch.30

rights.title: Judicial Review — Challenging the Lawfulness of Detention
rights.summary: If the section itself was procedurally unlawful — wrong form, wrong doctor, wrong process — you can challenge the legality of the detention in the High Court.
rights.detail: Judicial review is distinct from a Tribunal. The Tribunal asks 'should you be detained now?' — Judicial review asks 'was the detention lawful from the start?' Grounds include: wrong signatories on section papers, AMHP conflict of interest, nearest relative not consulted where required, section forms containing material errors, failure of Hospital Managers to review detention properly. A successful judicial review can result in a writ of habeas corpus (immediate release).
rights.howToExercise: Only through a specialist mental health law solicitor. An emergency application (urgent judicial review) can be made in hours if detention is clearly unlawful. Your IMHA can help contact appropriate solicitors. Legal aid is available.
rights.legalBasis: Senior Courts Act 1981, s.31; R (von Brandenburg) v East London NHS Foundation Trust [2003] UKHL 58
appliesTo: All detained patients where procedural error exists

dischargeRoutes.title: Mental Health Tribunal
dischargeRoutes.summary: An independent judicial body with the legal power to order your discharge. The burden of proof is on the hospital — they must prove detention is still justified.
dischargeRoutes.timeframe: s.2: hearing within 7 days. s.3 / CTO: hearing within 8 weeks.
steps: Contact a solicitor immediately — legal aid is automatic and free, no means test
Your solicitor makes a written application to the First-tier Tribunal (Mental Health)
The Tribunal will obtain reports from your Responsible Clinician and nursing staff
You (or your solicitor) can submit your own evidence — written statements, letters from family, care plans
A Tribunal panel of three (judge, doctor, lay member) holds a hearing — usually on the ward
The hospital must prove that ALL three criteria for detention are CURRENTLY met
If the hospital cannot satisfy the Tribunal on any one criterion, you MUST be discharged
outcomes: Absolute discharge — released immediately
Conditional discharge — released with conditions (e.g. medication compliance, regular outpatient contact, residence)
Deferred discharge — discharged on a specified future date or when named conditions are met (e.g. housing arranged)
Recommendation for leave, transfer to less restrictive setting, or consideration for CTO
No discharge — but RC report and hearing create valuable evidence for the next application
strengths: Legally binding — hospital MUST comply with the order
Burden of proof is on the hospital, not on you
Free legal aid — no cost to patient
Truly independent of the NHS trust
Can order conditional or deferred discharge when absolute is not yet possible
limitations: Takes time — up to 8 weeks for s.3, which can feel like an eternity
Absolute discharge granted in approximately 10–15% of hearings; conditional/deferred more common
Can only consider whether criteria are CURRENTLY met — not whether section was initially wrong
s.2 window is only 14 days — easy to miss
dischargeRoutes.tip: Apply on day one, even if uncertain. You can withdraw later. Use the preparation time to gather evidence. Ask your IMHA to help compile a personal statement for the panel.

dischargeRoutes.title: Hospital Managers' Hearing
dischargeRoutes.summary: An independent panel of non-executive directors who can discharge you even if your clinical team disagrees. Underused and more flexible than the Tribunal.
dischargeRoutes.timeframe: Usually arranged within 2–4 weeks of request.
dischargeRoutes.legalBasis: Mental Health Act 1983, s.23(2)(b); Code of Practice ch.38
steps: Write to the MHA Administration Office (or ask ward staff for the form) requesting a Hospital Managers' Hearing
You do not need a solicitor, but you may bring one — also consider asking your IMHA to attend
The panel reads reports from your clinical team; you can also submit your own written statement
The hearing is less formal than a Tribunal — you can speak directly to the panel
The panel asks the clinical team questions and hears your account
Panel votes on whether to discharge — a majority is sufficient
outcomes: Discharge — legally binding, immediate effect
No discharge — but you can request another hearing (though not immediately)
Panel may make recommendations to the clinical team about care planning, leave, or conditions
strengths: Can discharge even if the RC actively opposes it
More informal and accessible than a Tribunal
You can request one at any time (subject to reasonable frequency)
Panel members are lay people — often more empathetic to patient perspective
Useful after a Tribunal has declined discharge — different body, different view
limitations: Panels sometimes defer to clinical team opinion in practice
No legal aid (though you can bring your own solicitor or IMHA)
Decision reasons do not have to be given in full
Cannot make conditions — discharge is usually absolute or nothing
dischargeRoutes.tip: Request a Managers' hearing in writing, and simultaneously apply to the Tribunal. These are separate routes and both can run at the same time.

dischargeRoutes.title: Nearest Relative Discharge (s.23 Notice)
dischargeRoutes.summary: Your Nearest Relative can send a written notice ordering the Hospital Managers to discharge you. This starts a 72-hour clock during which the RC can only block it by filing a formal 'barring report'.
dischargeRoutes.timeframe: Discharge effective after 72 hours unless a s.25 barring report is filed.
steps: Identify your Nearest Relative using the s.26 hierarchy (see MHA Sections tab)
NR sends a signed written notice to the Hospital Managers / MHA Administration office
Send by recorded delivery and keep a copy — the 72-hour clock starts on receipt
If no s.25 report is filed by the RC within 72 hours, you are discharged
If RC files a s.25 report ('dangerous to self or others'), discharge is blocked for 6 months
After a s.25 barring, your NR can apply to the Tribunal within 28 days
outcomes: Discharge if no s.25 barring report filed within 72 hours
If barred: NR can apply to Tribunal — valuable as NR's application is counted separately to patient's own application
s.25 bar lasts 6 months — NR cannot give another notice during this period
strengths: No hearing needed — can work quickly (72 hours)
Does not use the patient's own Tribunal allocation
NR's Tribunal application (after s.25 bar) runs separately to yours
Useful tactical tool — especially near the end of a section period
limitations: Does not apply to s.2 (assessment only) — only s.3 and CTO
Requires a willing and identified NR (may be complicated if NR is absent or estranged)
RC often files s.25 within hours if they want to keep you — barring is common for patients who are being managed under s.3
If barred, the 6-month lockout prevents NR from giving another notice
dischargeRoutes.tip: Consider the timing carefully. A notice given near an RC review or planned discharge meeting is less likely to trigger a s.25 bar. Ask your solicitor to advise on the optimal moment.

dischargeRoutes.title: Clinical Discharge by Responsible Clinician
dischargeRoutes.summary: Your RC can discharge you at any time. Building a collaborative relationship, demonstrating insight, and having a robust community care plan in place are the core strategies.
dischargeRoutes.timeframe: No fixed timeline — depends on clinical progress and care planning.
steps: Engage actively with your ward round and care programme approach (CPA) meetings
Ask your IMHA to advocate for your perspective in clinical meetings
Work with your social worker to build a concrete community plan: named housing, CPN, medication, crisis line
Demonstrate insight — not necessarily accepting the diagnosis, but showing understanding of your situation and how to manage
Discuss a step-down plan: section 17 leave (community leave) → extended leave → CTO → discharge
Request a clear written care plan with discharge criteria so you know exactly what is expected of you
outcomes: Discharge — absolute or conditional (CTO)
Increased s.17 leave as intermediate step
Transfer to a less restrictive or closer-to-home ward
strengths: Quickest route if clinical team agrees
Not confrontational — preserves therapeutic relationship
RC can discharge at any time, even before a Tribunal date
Community leave (s.17) can provide meaningful freedom while formal discharge is prepared
limitations: Entirely dependent on clinical team's view — RC may be resistant
Can feel slow and uncertain without clear milestones
Patients perceived as lacking insight or refusing treatment face significant barriers
dischargeRoutes.tip: Ask for written discharge criteria in your care plan. If the team cannot articulate what would lead to discharge, that itself is a problem worth raising at the Tribunal.

dischargeRoutes.title: Community Treatment Order (CTO) as a Step Down
dischargeRoutes.summary: A CTO is not full discharge, but it moves you from 24-hour inpatient detention into the community — with conditions. It is a meaningful step toward freedom and a platform from which to seek absolute discharge.
dischargeRoutes.timeframe: CTO lasts 6 months initially, renewable. Can be used at any stage of s.3.
steps: Ask your RC and AMHP to consider a CTO at your next ward round
CTO conditions must be individually justified — challenge any conditions that seem excessive
Once on CTO, continue engaging with community services and keep all appointments
Apply to the Tribunal during the CTO period (you have a right to apply once per CTO period)
If you comply with all conditions and remain stable, petition the RC to revoke the CTO and discharge you absolutely
Conditions can be varied or removed over time — ask for a review
outcomes: Community living with conditions
Path toward absolute discharge if CTO period is stable
Tribunal can discharge from CTO (with or without conditions)
strengths: Immediate practical improvement — you are no longer an inpatient
Preserves family relationships and employment
Shows tribunal and clinical team you can manage in the community
Conditions can be gradually reduced toward absolute discharge
limitations: Not true freedom — recall to hospital if conditions breached
Some conditions can feel intrusive (e.g. random home visits, depot injections)
Recall is quick and does not require a full assessment — can feel precarious
dischargeRoutes.tip: CTO conditions must be necessary and proportionate to the risks. If a condition seems disproportionate, challenge it via the Tribunal or ask the AMHP to vary it.

dischargeRoutes.title: Judicial Review / Habeas Corpus
dischargeRoutes.summary: If the section itself was procedurally unlawful — wrong forms, wrong doctors, wrong process — the High Court can declare the detention unlawful and order immediate release.
applies: All sections where procedural error exists
dischargeRoutes.timeframe: Emergency application can be heard within hours. Standard JR: weeks.
dischargeRoutes.legalBasis: Senior Courts Act 1981, s.31; MHA 1983 (procedural requirements)
steps: Contact a specialist mental health law solicitor immediately
Solicitor checks the section papers for procedural errors: wrong form, missing signatures, wrong doctor status, AMHP conflicts of interest, NR not consulted where required
If an error is found, solicitor applies for judicial review (or habeas corpus for immediate release)
An emergency application can be heard the same day if detention is obviously unlawful
If JR succeeds, the court quashes the section and orders release — or the hospital may concede and discharge
outcomes: Writ of habeas corpus — immediate release if detention is unlawful
Quashing of the section — further detention requires a fresh, valid section
Compensation claims possible if unlawful detention proven
strengths: Can succeed regardless of your mental state — procedural errors are black-and-white
Emergency applications move extremely fast
Hospital often concedes and discharges rather than defend a clearly flawed section
limitations: Requires a specific procedural error — not available simply because you disagree with the section
Requires specialist legal expertise
Successful JR does not prevent re-sectioning if clinical grounds still exist
dischargeRoutes.tip: Always have a solicitor check the section paperwork immediately on admission. Errors are more common than people realise — AMHP qualification, doctor independence, form completion, and NR consultation are the most frequent problem areas.

patientCircumstances.title: Previous Detentions or Hospital Admissions
patientCircumstances.description: A history of previous detentions creates a documented pattern that RCs and Tribunal panels use to argue that the illness is chronic and that discharge risks relapse. The more previous admissions, the harder the argument becomes — but it is not insurmountable.

strategies.action: Document exactly what is different this time
strategies.detail: Be specific: what circumstances caused the previous admissions? Are those circumstances now resolved? Has your social support changed? Do not rely on vague statements like 'I am better now' — the Tribunal wants concrete, specific evidence of changed circumstances.

strategies.action: Get your Subject Access Records before the Tribunal
strategies.detail: Request your full medical records (SAR) immediately. Inaccurate or outdated information in previous reports can seriously harm your case. If records contain errors, formally request correction under UK GDPR Art.16 and raise the inaccuracies in your Tribunal statement.

strategies.action: Highlight periods of stability between admissions
strategies.detail: A 'revolving door' pattern looks different if there were long periods of stable community living. If you lived in the community for 2+ years between admissions, document this with evidence (GP letters, housing, employment).

strategies.action: Develop a detailed relapse prevention plan
strategies.detail: Work with your care team (or independently with your IMHA) to create a written relapse prevention plan that names specific early warning signs, who to contact, and what action to take. Present this to the Tribunal.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: RC reports in Tribunal proceedings almost always describe previous admissions extensively. Your solicitor should specifically address each previous admission in their submissions — not ignore them.

patientCircumstances.title: History of Violence or Aggression on Records
patientCircumstances.description: Documented incidents of violence, assault, or threatening behaviour are the single most significant factor in Tribunal decisions. The 'danger to others' limb of the discharge test weighs heavily on any such history. This does not make discharge impossible, but it requires careful preparation.

strategies.action: Obtain and analyse the specific incident documentation
strategies.detail: Request the incident reports via SAR. Understand exactly what is documented, when it occurred, what the context was, and whether the records are accurate. Challenge inaccuracies formally in writing before the Tribunal.

strategies.action: Contextualise incidents relative to current mental state
strategies.detail: Incidents that occurred during acute psychosis or at a time of untreated illness are very different from incidents now. Your solicitor can argue that with treatment and insight, the specific risk factors that caused previous incidents are no longer present.

strategies.action: Commission an independent risk assessment
strategies.detail: Your solicitor can arrange for an independent forensic psychologist or psychiatrist to conduct a risk assessment. An independent view that risk is manageable in the community can directly counter the RC's risk assessment.

strategies.action: Build a community risk management plan
strategies.detail: Create a concrete plan: named CPN, weekly appointments, crisis numbers, named individuals who will monitor warning signs, and what those signs are. A robust plan reassures the Tribunal that risk can be managed outside hospital.

strategies.action: Evidence of therapeutic work on the incident
strategies.detail: Have you completed any anger management, dialectical behaviour therapy, or victim empathy work? Evidence of this significantly shifts the Tribunal's view of future risk.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: Attempting to minimise or deny historical incidents reads very badly to Tribunal panels. Acknowledging what happened while explaining the context and what has changed is a far more effective strategy.

patientCircumstances.title: History of Non-Compliance with Medication or Treatment
patientCircumstances.description: If records show that you have previously stopped medication, refused treatment, or disengaged from services — leading to relapse — the Tribunal and clinical team will treat this as a significant risk factor. However, non-compliance has context that can be explained.

strategies.action: Explain WHY non-compliance occurred
strategies.detail: Stopping medication because of intolerable side effects (weight gain, sedation, sexual dysfunction, tardive dyskinesia) is very different from stopping because you did not believe you were ill. Document the specific side effects and request a medication review or alternatives.

strategies.action: Request a medication review or alternatives
strategies.detail: Ask the RC for a formal medication review. Different medications, depot (long-acting injection), or lower doses may address the side effect problems. Engaging with this process demonstrates willingness.

strategies.action: Offer a CTO as a middle ground
strategies.detail: A CTO with a medication condition may be offered or can be requested. This can be presented to the Tribunal as an alternative to continued inpatient detention — demonstrating that you are willing to take medication in the community.

strategies.action: Show current engagement
strategies.detail: Attendance at ward rounds, engagement with OT, participation in therapeutic groups — all of this is documented. Current engagement patterns can offset historical non-compliance.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: Saying 'I will take medication' without specific evidence of current compliance is not convincing. The Tribunal looks at behaviour, not promises.

patientCircumstances.title: Substance Misuse or Dual Diagnosis
patientCircumstances.description: A history of drug or alcohol misuse — especially if linked to previous relapses or the current admission — significantly affects Tribunal hearings and clinical discharge decisions. Dual diagnosis (mental illness and substance misuse) requires evidence of concurrent management.

strategies.action: Request a formal referral to dual diagnosis services
strategies.detail: Ask your RC or social worker to refer you to the trust's dual diagnosis or substance misuse team. Engagement with this team creates documented evidence that you are taking the issue seriously.

strategies.action: Obtain evidence of community support engagement
strategies.detail: AA, NA, SMART Recovery, or local drug and alcohol service attendance letters are powerful evidence. Even a few attended sessions with a letter on headed paper makes a significant difference at Tribunal.

strategies.action: Agree to drug testing as part of CTO conditions if helpful
strategies.detail: Proactively offering urine drug testing as a CTO condition can reassure the Tribunal and RC. It demonstrates confidence in your own abstinence and willingness to be accountable.

strategies.action: Establish a relapse prevention plan for substance use
strategies.detail: A written plan identifying triggers, named support contacts, and what to do if temptation arises — separate from but linked to the mental health relapse prevention plan — shows the Tribunal that you have a realistic strategy.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: Minimising substance use history ('it wasn't that much' / 'I can handle it now') is consistently ineffective. Acknowledging the issue and showing what you have done about it is far more credible.

patientCircumstances.title: Criminal History (No Restriction Order)
patientCircumstances.description: Previous criminal convictions can colour clinical team risk perceptions and appear in Tribunal reports — even if they are old, spent, or entirely unrelated to mental health. However, the Tribunal must apply the MHA test, not make moral character judgments.

strategies.action: Ensure your solicitor separates criminal history from MHA criteria
strategies.detail: The Tribunal's job is to determine whether the MHA criteria are currently met — not to evaluate your character. Your solicitor should explicitly address this in submissions if the RC report conflates past offending with current mental health risk.

strategies.action: Highlight rehabilitation evidence
strategies.detail: Completion of community payback, probation compliance, offending behaviour programmes, or simply many years without offending all demonstrate that past behaviour is not a reliable predictor of future risk.

strategies.action: Distinguish the offending context from your current situation
strategies.detail: Was the offending during a period of untreated mental illness? During addiction? At a very different life stage? Contextualising past behaviour is legitimate and important.

strategies.action: Request spent convictions are not cited
strategies.detail: Under the Rehabilitation of Offenders Act, spent convictions should not be given undue weight. Ask your solicitor to challenge excessive reliance on old or spent convictions in RC reports.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: Criminal history and mental health status are legally distinct. Do not allow clinical reports to conflate the two without challenge.

patientCircumstances.title: Restricted Patient (Hospital Order + Restriction Order, s.37/41)
patientCircumstances.description: If you were sentenced by a Crown Court and given a hospital order with a restriction order (s.41), your discharge is governed by completely different rules. The Ministry of Justice (Secretary of State) must be involved in any discharge decision, and the usual patient-initiated routes are more limited.

strategies.action: Apply to the Tribunal annually
strategies.detail: Restricted patients can apply to the Tribunal once a year (not just once per detention period). The Tribunal can grant conditional discharge for restricted patients — which is realistically the first step for most. Apply every year without fail.

strategies.action: Build a progressive rehabilitation record
strategies.detail: The Ministry of Justice looks at your progression through the hospital system. Movement from high to medium to low security, increasing levels of leave (s.17), and a consistent record of compliance all build the MoJ's confidence to support discharge.

strategies.action: Engage with the forensic team and probation
strategies.detail: Your forensic team will have a named MoJ case manager. Maintaining a positive working relationship with this team is essential — their reports go directly to the Secretary of State. Probation will also be involved in any conditional discharge plan.

strategies.action: Work toward conditional discharge as the realistic first step
strategies.detail: Absolute discharge for restricted patients is rare. Conditional discharge with supervision, residence requirements, and treatment conditions is the realistic first goal. Show the Tribunal you have a robust community plan.

strategies.action: Use a specialist forensic mental health law solicitor
strategies.detail: Restricted patient law is highly specialised. A solicitor with specific expertise in restricted patients and MoJ proceedings is essential — this is not general mental health law.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: The usual nearest-relative discharge and Hospital Managers' discharge routes do not apply to restricted patients — the restriction order suspends these powers.

patientCircumstances.title: Lack of Insight (Not Accepting the Diagnosis)
patientCircumstances.description: Lack of insight is the most frequently cited reason for refusing discharge at Tribunal and for RC opposition. It is not a formal criterion for detention, but it is treated as evidence that the illness is ongoing and that the patient would not take medication or seek help in the community.

strategies.action: Distinguish between accepting diagnosis and accepting illness
strategies.detail: You do not have to agree with the specific diagnosis to demonstrate insight. Insight means: acknowledging that your mental health has caused problems; recognising warning signs; being willing to seek help. You can say 'I disagree with the diagnosis but I accept that I have been very unwell and that I need support' — this is infinitely better than flat denial.

strategies.action: Focus on behaviour, not belief
strategies.detail: The Tribunal responds better to 'I will attend outpatient appointments, take prescribed medication, and contact the crisis team if I feel unwell' than to any argument about the diagnosis. Demonstrating willingness to comply is more persuasive than agreeing with the label.

strategies.action: Use your IMHA to help articulate your perspective
strategies.detail: An IMHA can help you express your views in a way that is constructive rather than confrontational. They can attend ward rounds with you to advocate for your perspective and help you phrase your position effectively.

strategies.action: Prepare a written personal statement for the Tribunal
strategies.detail: A written statement acknowledging what happened, expressing concern for your own wellbeing, and setting out your community plan is often more persuasive than anything said verbally in the hearing. Your solicitor will help draft this.

strategies.action: Challenge 'insight' as a criterion if it is being used unfairly
strategies.detail: Insight is not one of the three legal criteria in s.72 — the Tribunal must discharge if the criteria are not met, regardless of insight. If the Tribunal or RC is treating lack of insight as a standalone reason for detention, your solicitor can challenge this legally.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: Engaging in extended arguments about your diagnosis during Tribunal hearings almost always backfires. The panel interprets this as lack of insight, not as evidence of capacity. Save diagnostic disagreements for clinical settings and focus Tribunal time on your community plan.

patientCircumstances.title: No Stable Housing or Community Support
patientCircumstances.description: Lack of housing or community support is NOT a legal ground for detention under the MHA. However, in practice, Tribunals regularly defer discharge until these are arranged — treating a social care failure as a clinical necessity. This is legally challengeable.

strategies.action: Raise the s.117 duty proactively and in writing
strategies.detail: The NHS and local authority have a statutory duty under s.117 to arrange aftercare BEFORE discharge from s.3. If housing or support has not been arranged, this is a breach of s.117 — not a reason to keep you detained. Write to the Trust and local authority stating that their s.117 duty is unfulfilled.

strategies.action: Request an urgent CPA/aftercare meeting
strategies.detail: Ask your social worker to convene an urgent Care Programme Approach (CPA) meeting focused on discharge planning. Minutes of this meeting, which should include housing referrals and care package details, can be presented at the Tribunal as evidence of active planning.

strategies.action: Challenge deferred discharge at the Tribunal
strategies.detail: If the Tribunal is considering deferring discharge until housing is arranged, ask your solicitor to argue that lack of housing is a local authority failure — not a clinical reason for continued detention. The Tribunal can order discharge and the local authority must then act.

strategies.action: Make housing referrals directly
strategies.detail: As a detained patient you have priority need under the Housing Act 1996. Write to the local authority housing department directly (or ask your social worker or IMHA). A local authority housing assessment letter presented to the Tribunal demonstrates active progress.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: s.117 aftercare is FREE — the local authority cannot charge for it. If anyone suggests that services will only be available if you pay, this is unlawful. IMHA can help you challenge this.

patientCircumstances.title: Absent, Estranged, or Unhelpful Nearest Relative
patientCircumstances.description: The Nearest Relative discharge route (s.23 NR notice) requires a willing and capable NR. If yours is estranged, deceased, abroad, unwilling, or actively hostile to your interests, this route is blocked — but other routes remain fully open.

strategies.action: Check the full s.26 hierarchy
strategies.detail: The hierarchy is long: spouse/partner, child, parent, sibling, grandparent, grandchild, uncle/aunt, nephew/niece, other relative. If the first person is unavailable, the next in line takes the role. Work through the full list before concluding you have no accessible NR.

strategies.action: Apply to displace an unhelpful NR under s.29
strategies.detail: If your NR is acting against your interests — for example, supporting your detention when you believe it is unjustified — the AMHP or a relative can apply to the County Court to displace them. The court appoints a more appropriate person. This takes time but is available.

strategies.action: Use Tribunal and Managers' routes instead
strategies.detail: The Tribunal and Hospital Managers' hearing do not require NR involvement. These routes are fully open regardless of your NR situation and are generally more powerful anyway.

strategies.action: Nominate a trusted person to be kept informed
strategies.detail: Even if the NR discharge route is unavailable, you can ask for a named person (friend, advocate) to be kept informed of your treatment. This is not a legal NR role but gives you a trusted ally in the process.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: If your NR is in a position to object to a s.3 admission (and they reasonably do), the AMHP cannot proceed without applying to court to displace them. This is actually a protection as much as it can be an obstacle.

patientCircumstances.title: First Admission — No Prior History
patientCircumstances.description: A first-time detention, with no prior history of psychiatric admission, is actually advantageous at Tribunal and in clinical discharge discussions. There is no 'revolving door' pattern, and the episode can be contextualised as a response to specific acute circumstances that have since changed.

strategies.action: Contextualise the admission explicitly
strategies.detail: Was there a specific trigger — bereavement, relationship breakdown, sleep deprivation, drug use, extreme stress? If so, document what that trigger was, that it has now resolved, and what protective factors exist against recurrence.

strategies.action: Emphasise the absence of a pattern
strategies.detail: The Tribunal is weighing risk based partly on past behaviour. The absence of past admissions is evidence in your favour — explicitly make this argument. 'This is my first admission; there is no pattern of revolving-door admission' is a valid and important point.

strategies.action: Use IMHA even if it seems unnecessary
strategies.detail: First-time patients often underestimate the complexity of the process. Patients who engage with an IMHA and solicitor at their first Tribunal have significantly better outcomes. Do not assume a clear case will resolve itself.

strategies.action: Set up robust community support proactively
strategies.detail: For a first admission with no existing community team, you may need to proactively arrange GP follow-up, private or NHS counselling, and any support that demonstrates you will not be isolated after discharge.

patientCircumstances.watchOut: Even first-time patients can be kept on s.2 or s.3 if the acute episode is severe. Being a first admission does not automatically mean the Tribunal discharges. The legal criteria still apply.

ui.heading: Getting Out — Discharge Pathways
ui.subheading: Six routes to discharge, ranked by power. Pursue multiple routes simultaneously. Use the Circumstances tab for strategies tailored to your records.
ui.urgentBody: On s.2 you have only 14 days to apply to the Tribunal — this cannot be extended. Contact a mental health law solicitor immediately. Legal aid is free, no means test.
